summary: Second broad cleanup pass replacing easily matching fighter-object access patterns with the `GET_FIGHTER(gobj)` macro across fighter and character-specific source files. The PR touched 67 files, mostly under `src/melee/ft/chara`, converting direct `fighter_gobj->user_data`, casts such as `(Fighter*) fighter_gobj->user_data`, and some `getFighter(fighter_gobj)` uses to the project macro where matching allowed. Some special-attribute accesses were also adjusted away from `getFtSpecialAttrs`/related inline helpers to direct `fp->x2D4_specialAttributes` access, with `MUST_MATCH` unused-stack padding added or resized where necessary. The author explicitly noted this was not yet at parity with PR #614 and depended on PR #711.
what_changed: Replaced many direct fighter pointer loads from `fighter_gobj->user_data` with `GET_FIGHTER(fighter_gobj)`.
Replaced casted forms such as `Fighter* fp = (Fighter*) fighter_gobj->user_data;` with `Fighter* fp = GET_FIGHTER(fighter_gobj);`.
Replaced at least some existing `getFighter(fighter_gobj)` calls with `GET_FIGHTER(fighter_gobj)` in Donkey Kong special-move code.

Applied the macro conversion across a large set of character modules including Captain, Donkey, Fox, Game & Watch, Luigi, Mewtwo, Ness, Pikachu, Samus, Zelda, Yoshi, Kirby, Link, Ice Climbers, Purin, Crazy Hand, and Giga Koopa files.

Adjusted some special-attribute retrievals from inline helpers such as `getFtSpecialAttrs(fp)` or `getFtSpecialAttrsD(...)` to direct `fp->x2D4_specialAttributes` access when the inline interfered with matching.
Added, removed, or resized `#ifdef MUST_MATCH` unused stack arrays in several functions to preserve matching after macro and attribute-access changes.
Touched fighter-common files such as `fighter.c`, `ftcoll.c`, `ftlib.c`, `ftlib.h`, `ftwalkcommon.c`, and `ft_unknown_006.c`, though the provided diff evidence is strongest for the character files.
smart_moves: Limited the pass to 'easily matching' replacements rather than forcing every possible `user_data` access through the macro, reducing risk in sensitive decomp code.
Used `GET_FIGHTER` to make the intended type and access pattern explicit without changing runtime behavior.
Recognized that certain inline attribute helpers affected matching and locally replaced them with direct field access instead of abandoning the macro conversion.
Kept match-only stack artifacts behind `#ifdef MUST_MATCH`, making the matching workaround explicit and separable from normal source intent.
Documented questionable stack workarounds with `/// @todo Unused stack.` comments, preserving future cleanup targets.
Handled stack padding sizes granularly, including shrinking or removing obsolete unused arrays where the macro conversion changed stack layout.
decomp_lessons: For fighter GObj access, prefer `GET_FIGHTER(fighter_gobj)` over raw `fighter_gobj->user_data` when it matches; it standardizes decompiled code and encodes the expected type.
Macro standardization in matched decomp code can alter register allocation or stack layout, so every substitution still needs build/match verification.

Inline helper functions such as `getFtSpecialAttrs` may not be match-neutral in all contexts; direct field access like `fp->x2D4_specialAttributes` may be necessary until the underlying attribute model is better understood.
When macro cleanup exposes or changes unused stack requirements, keep stack padding guarded by `MUST_MATCH` and document it as a temporary matching artifact.
Large mechanical cleanup passes are safer when scoped to cases that already match easily and when exceptions are left for later investigation.
Special-attribute access is a known difficult area because of inline behavior and unresolved state-variable/attribute-struct issues; future work should treat those helpers carefully.
naming_conventions: `GET_FIGHTER(fighter_gobj)` is the preferred macro spelling for retrieving `Fighter* fp` from an `HSD_GObj*` in fighter code.
The common local variable name for the retrieved fighter pointer remains `fp`.
Character special attribute locals follow character-specific names such as `donkey_attr` and `captainAttrs`.
Direct special-attribute access used the field name `fp->x2D4_specialAttributes` in cases where helper inlines were problematic.
Match-only unused stack arrays were named `unused` and guarded with `#ifdef MUST_MATCH`.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Converted raw `user_data` loads to `GET_FIGHTER` only where matching was straightforward.
Replaced problematic `getFtSpecialAttrs(fp)`/related inline calls with direct `fp->x2D4_specialAttributes` access in several special-move functions.
Inserted `u8 unused[8]`, `u8 unused[4]`, or similar `MUST_MATCH`-guarded arrays to maintain stack layout after refactors.
Reduced some existing unused stack arrays, for example changing `u8 unused[16]` to `u8 unused[8]` and `u8 unused[12]` to `u8 unused[4]` in Donkey Kong special code.
Removed obsolete `MUST_MATCH` unused stack padding in some Donkey Kong collision/action transition callbacks once it was no longer needed.
Reviewer discussion confirms the attribute-helper inline could cause `GET_FIGHTER` not to match in cases where it otherwise should.

review_feedback.author: r-burns
review_feedback.comment: Asked why the PR changed `getFtSpecialAttrs(fp)` to direct `fp->x2D4_specialAttributes` and added unused stack padding.

review_feedback.author: ribbanya
review_feedback.comment: Explained that something was wrong with the inline causing `GET_FIGHTER` not to match where it should; suggested deprecating the helper until fighter attributes and state-variable union issues are better understood.

follow_up_queries: Find remaining `fighter_gobj->user_data` accesses that were not converted to `GET_FIGHTER` after PR #712.
Investigate why `getFtSpecialAttrs` and related inline helpers alter matching when combined with `GET_FIGHTER`.
Audit all direct `fp->x2D4_specialAttributes` replacements to see whether a safer matching helper can be introduced.
Compare PR #711, PR #712, and PR #614 to identify remaining gaps in GET_FIGHTER macro adoption.
Search for `/// @todo Unused stack.` in fighter files to prioritize later cleanup once matching constraints are better understood.
